meta.name: Curated Quests
meta.description: Hand-authored, zero-AI study quests. Each quest is a depth-gated thread: read, find, connect, reflect. Tradition-tagged so the runtime never assumes one canon.

steps.prompt: Read the covenant with Noah. Note the sign God gives and to whom it extends.
steps.reveal: The Noahic covenant is unconditional and universal — it binds 'every living creature', not one people. The bow is laid down, a weapon unstrung.

steps.prompt: Read the covenant with Abram. Watch who passes between the pieces.
steps.reveal: Only God (the smoking firepot) passes between the halves — Abram sleeps. The covenant's burden falls on God alone.

steps.prompt: Connect the sign of circumcision (ch.17) back to the reckoning of righteousness (15:6). Which came first — the faith or the sign?
steps.reveal: Righteousness is credited in ch.15 before the sign is given in ch.17. The sign seals a faith already present.

steps.prompt: Find where the covenant is re-spoken to Isaac, then to Jacob. List the three recipients in order.
steps.reveal: Abraham → Isaac → Jacob. The covenant is dynastic: it descends, gathering the same threefold promise (land, seed, blessing-to-nations).

steps.prompt: Joseph dies in Egypt invoking the promise. Write one line on how a covenant survives outside the promised land.
steps.reveal: The covenant is carried in memory and bones, not geography. 'God will surely visit you' — the thread outlives every exile.

steps.prompt: Read the divine name given to Moses. Hold the Greek of the LXX (ego eimi) in mind for what follows.
steps.reveal: 'I AM THAT I AM' — ehyeh asher ehyeh. The Septuagint renders the absolute 'I AM' as ego eimi, the exact phrase John will weaponize.

steps.prompt: Find the predicated 'I AM' sayings: bread, light, door, shepherd, resurrection. Note each predicate.
steps.reveal: Each predicate is a self-disclosure: sustenance, guidance, access, sacrifice, life-over-death. Seven in total across the gospel.

steps.prompt: Find the final two predicated sayings: the way/truth/life, and the true vine.
steps.reveal: These close the set: relationship (the way) and abiding (the vine). The seven 'I AM' predicates form a complete christology.

steps.prompt: Connect the absolute 'Before Abraham was, I AM' to Exodus 3:14. Why do they pick up stones in the next verse?
steps.reveal: 8:58 drops the predicate — it is the bare divine name. The crowd hears blasphemy and reaches for stones (Lev 24:16). The claim is unmistakable.

steps.prompt: At the arrest, when Jesus says 'ego eimi', the soldiers fall to the ground. Write one line on this final, wordless 'I AM'.
steps.reveal: The same name that called Israel out of Egypt knocks an armed cohort flat — then surrenders to them anyway. Power held in restraint.

steps.prompt: Read the catch of fish. The number is exact and unexplained. Why count them?
steps.reveal: 153 large fish, 'and the net was not torn'. A number this specific in a symbolic gospel invites a second reading.

steps.prompt: Compute: 153 is the sum of 1..17 (the 17th triangular number). Run a gematria match in your own library for any term valuing 153.
steps.reveal: 153 = the triangular number of 17. In Hebrew, 'bnei ha-Elohim' (sons of God) and 'Ani Elohim' (I am God) have been read at this value by various hands — the index is the place to check, not to assume.

steps.prompt: Connect the fish of John 21 to the fishermen of Ezekiel's river. From En-gedi to En-eglaim, 'fish of very many kinds'.
steps.reveal: Ezekiel's healed sea teems with every kind of fish, fishermen spreading nets — John's unbroken net gathering 153 may echo this universal harvest.

steps.prompt: Gematria reveals associations, not proofs. Write one line distinguishing a pattern you find compelling from one you find forced.
steps.reveal: The discipline of esoteric reading is restraint: a value that recurs across independent witnesses is a thread; a value you had to strain to reach is your own.

steps.prompt: Read the protoevangelium — the first promise of a bruised heel and a crushed head.
steps.reveal: The 'seed of the woman' against the serpent. Read by many as the earliest messianic thread, before any law or temple.

steps.prompt: Connect the Immanuel sign to Matthew's citation. Note what Matthew claims it fulfills.
steps.reveal: Matthew reads Isaiah's near-term sign as also a far-term fulfillment — 'God with us' realized in the nativity.

steps.prompt: Find the birthplace prophecy and where the scribes quote it to Herod.
steps.reveal: Bethlehem Ephrathah, 'least among the clans', named centuries before. The scribes know the text cold — yet do not follow the star.

steps.prompt: Find the Suffering Servant song. List three afflictions the Servant bears for others.
steps.reveal: Wounded for transgressions, crushed for iniquities, the chastisement that brings peace — substitution at the heart of the song.

steps.prompt: Connect the pierced hands and divided garments of Psalm 22 to the crucifixion in John.
steps.reveal: Casting lots for clothing, pierced extremities — John reads the passion as Psalm 22 enacted. Written before crucifixion existed as a method.

steps.prompt: On the Emmaus road, 'beginning with Moses', the whole thread is read at once. Write one line on reading a library backwards from its end.
steps.reveal: A thread is only visible once you know where it leads. The risen reader rereads the scroll and the scattered signs become one line.

meta.title: The Names of God in Exodus

steps.prompt: Read the revelation of the Name at the bush. Note the tense and the refusal to be pinned down.
steps.reveal: Ehyeh asher ehyeh — 'I will be what I will be', a verb, not a noun. The Name resists capture; it is a promise of presence, not a definition.

steps.prompt: Find where God says the patriarchs knew El Shaddai but not the Name YHWH. Hold the tension with Genesis.
steps.reveal: A classic source-critical and theological crux: the Name's full disclosure is reserved for the exodus generation — redemption unlocks revelation.

steps.prompt: Connect the Name to its own self-description after the golden calf. List the attributes proclaimed.
steps.reveal: Compassionate, gracious, slow to anger, abounding in hesed and truth — the Thirteen Attributes, recited in liturgy to this day. The Name is defined by mercy.

steps.prompt: The Shema gathers the Name into one. Write one line on what it means to call the unnameable 'one'.
steps.reveal: Unity is not arithmetic here but allegiance — the Name that will-be is the only one to whom the heart is wholly bound.

steps.prompt: Read Wisdom's song. She is present at creation, 'beside him like a master worker'.
steps.reveal: Wisdom (Hokhmah) is pre-existent, rejoicing in the inhabited world. A feminine figure standing at the origin of all things.

steps.prompt: Connect the Logos 'in the beginning, with God' to Wisdom 'beside him'. What did each help make?
steps.reveal: John maps the Logos onto the Wisdom tradition: both pre-exist, both are with God, both are the agent through whom all things are made.

steps.prompt: Find where Paul calls Christ the one in whom 'all things hold together'. Compare to Wisdom's role.
steps.reveal: 'Firstborn of all creation… all things hold together in him' — Wisdom's cosmic function, now christological. The thread runs through the wisdom literature into the hymns.

steps.prompt: Paul calls Christ 'the wisdom of God'. Write one line on a tradition reading its oldest poem into its newest figure.
steps.reveal: To name Christ as Wisdom is to claim the whole sapiential canon as a single, slowly-disclosed portrait.

meta.title: The Light Within: A Gnostic Reading of John

steps.prompt: Read the light that 'enlightens everyone'. Hold the dualism of light and darkness lightly.
steps.reveal: 'The light shines in the darkness' — the prologue's dualism gave gnostic readers a frame: an interior light, kindled from above, that the dark cannot grasp.

steps.prompt: Find 'the kingdom of God is within you' (or 'in your midst'). Note the translation tension.
steps.reveal: Entos hymon — 'within' or 'among'. Gnostic readers heard an interior kingdom; the Gospel of Thomas (log. 3) makes the same move: the kingdom is inside and outside you.

steps.prompt: Connect 'the truth will set you free' to gnosis as liberating knowledge. What kind of knowing frees?
steps.reveal: For the gnostic, salvation is recognition — knowing one's origin in the light. 'Free' is not pardon but awakening from forgetfulness.

steps.prompt: A gnostic reading is one lens among many. Write one line on what it illuminates and what it risks leaving in shadow.
steps.reveal: Interiority gains depth but can lose the body, the neighbor, and history. Every lens both reveals and occludes — name the trade.

steps.prompt: Read the departure from Rameses to Succoth. Open the map and locate the start.
steps.reveal: Rameses (Pi-Ramesse) in the eastern Nile delta. The route's first leg sets the southern-vs-northern-route debate that still divides scholars.

steps.prompt: Find why they avoid the 'way of the land of the Philistines'. Locate Pi-hahiroth and Migdol on the map.
steps.reveal: The coastal road was the fortified military highway — too dangerous. They turn toward the Sea of Reeds (Yam Suph), whose exact location is itself contested.

steps.prompt: Connect the arrival at Sinai to the wilderness-itinerary list of Numbers 33. Trace two waypoints.
steps.reveal: Numbers 33 preserves a 42-stage itinerary — a geographic memory-text. Cross-referencing it against the map turns narrative into a route.

steps.prompt: The map has gaps the text does not fill. Write one line on reading geography where the ground is uncertain.
steps.reveal: Sacred geography is part archaeology, part memory, part theology. Mark what is located, what is plausible, and what is lost — and keep them distinct.

steps.prompt: Read the command to speak of these words 'when you lie down and when you rise'. This is the seed of the first Mishnah.
steps.reveal: Berakhot 2a opens by asking: from when may one recite the evening Shema? The whole tractate grows from this single verse's 'when you lie down'.

steps.prompt: Find the question the Mishnah asks about timing. (From the verse alone: when does evening begin?)
steps.reveal: The Mishnah fixes it 'from the time the priests enter to eat their terumah' — a concrete daily marker. The Gemara then interrogates every word of that answer.

steps.prompt: Connect the daily rhythm of Shema to the priestly blessing. Both structure sacred time. How do they differ?
steps.reveal: The Shema is the people's twice-daily acceptance of the yoke of heaven; the blessing is the priest's bestowal. Reception versus gift — the Talmud lives in the gap between.

steps.prompt: A daf is a conversation across centuries. Write one line on entering an argument that has no last word.
steps.reveal: To learn a daf is to take a seat at a table where no one has finished speaking. You do not solve it; you join it.

steps.prompt: Read 'the Word became flesh'. This single clause is the seed of four centuries of councils.
steps.reveal: 'Kai ho logos sarx egeneto' — egeneto (became) is the contested verb. Did the Word change, or assume? The Fathers built their christology on this word.

steps.prompt: Connect the kenosis hymn ('emptied himself') to John 1:14. What is emptied, and what is kept?
steps.reveal: Athanasius read the 'emptying' as veiling, not loss: the Word does not cease to fill all things while becoming an infant. 'He became what we are that we might become what he is.'

steps.prompt: Find where Hebrews argues he had to be made like his brothers 'in every respect'. Why the insistence on full humanity?
steps.reveal: Gregory of Nazianzus: 'what is not assumed is not healed.' Hebrews' 'in every respect' becomes the Fathers' rule against any half-incarnation.

steps.prompt: The Fathers fought over a verb. Write one line on why the precise wording of a mystery still mattered to them.
steps.reveal: For the Fathers, getting the grammar wrong meant getting salvation wrong. Precision was not pedantry but pastoral care — the shape of the cure depended on the diagnosis.
